outlook.headline: AI-driven retimer and SerDes demand is broadening
outlook.summary: Demand is improving across the stack as AI rack-scale deployments pull in direct-attached copper, high-speed SerDes, linear equalizers, and PCIe retimers. The strongest reads are at Microchip, MaxLinear, Marvell, Credo, and Rambus, while Broadcom and Semtech also point to healthy pull-through; the main near-term watch is whether tight supply at the stronger names limits conversion rather than demand. Mention volume is only modestly above trend, so this looks like a real demand upturn rather than a hype spike.

drivers.label: AI rack-scale pull-through
drivers.detail: Large AI rack deployments are driving demand for direct-attached copper and high-speed SerDes content.

drivers.label: Hot retimer demand and capacity ramp
drivers.detail: Customers are explicitly requesting retimers and the company is ramping production and capacity to meet demand.

drivers.label: Healthy linear-equalizer / SerDes demand
drivers.detail: Better SerDes performance is lifting demand for linear equalizers and the broader product family.

drivers.label: Huge retimer opportunity
drivers.detail: The retimer opportunity is described as large now that the PHY challenge is effectively solved.

drivers.label: PCIe retimer and AI fabric traction
drivers.detail: Growing traction for PCIe retimer switch IP and expansion into broader AI fabric signal widening adoption.

drivers.label: Strong SerDes performance and tight supply
drivers.detail: Ramping revenue, strong SerDes performance, and tight supply indicate robust demand and favorable positioning.

drivers.label: Broad-based growth across speed grades
drivers.detail: Growth in 100G, 200G, and PCIe 6.0 retimers points to broadening demand across the portfolio.

risks: Tight supply could cap near-term revenue conversion at Microchip and Marvell if demand outpaces output.
If AI system architectures shift to fewer retimer or SerDes-heavy content, the current pull-through could moderate.
Mention momentum is only slightly above trend, so a near-term pause in AI infrastructure spending would be quickly visible.
